stories.headline: Israel and Lebanon announce conditional ceasefire as Hezbollah rejects truce and Israeli strikes continue
stories.body: Israel and Lebanon agreed in US-mediated talks to renew a fragile ceasefire and create pilot security zones in southern Lebanon where the Lebanese Armed Forces would exclude Hezbollah and other non-state actors, but Hezbollah’s leader Naim Qassem rejected the plan as a “roadmap to annihilate part of the Lebanese people” and demanded a full Israeli withdrawal. The deal is contingent on Hezbollah stopping attacks and evacuating operatives from the area between the Israeli border and the Litani River, while Israel’s defense minister said Israeli forces would continue operations in southern Lebanon for now. Fighting continued after the announcement, with Israeli strikes reported in southern Lebanon and the Bekaa Valley and Hezbollah claiming attacks on Israeli soldiers, raising doubts over whether Lebanon can implement a truce without Hezbollah’s consent.

stories.headline: Protests grow over planned US Ebola quarantine facility in Kenya as DRC outbreak response faces violence
stories.body: The Trump administration’s plan to build a 50-bed Ebola quarantine and treatment unit for Americans at Kenya’s Laikipia Air Base has triggered protests, legal challenges and criticism from former US health officials, CDC workers and Kenyan health unions, who say the policy departs from past practice of treating exposed US responders at home and risks treating Kenya as a containment site. A Kenyan court temporarily suspended the plan, but US and Kenyan preparations reportedly continued, prompting demonstrations in Nanyuki and demands that the facility be shut down. The controversy comes as an Ebola outbreak involving the Bundibugyo strain spreads in eastern DRC and Uganda, while attacks blamed on the Islamic State-linked ADF militia near Beni have killed more than 30 people, displaced residents and caused confirmed Ebola patients to flee treatment centers, complicating containment efforts.

stories.headline: Civilians flee Mogadishu as Somali troops and opposition-allied militias trade fire
stories.body: Heavy fighting broke out in Mogadishu between Somali government forces and militias aligned with opposition leaders after President Hassan Sheikh Mohamud extended his term by one year beyond its May 15 expiration, a move opponents called unconstitutional. Mortars and gunfire hit densely populated neighborhoods and the Bakara market area, forcing civilians to flee, while former president Sharif Sheikh Ahmed and former prime minister Hassan Ali Khaire accused government forces of attacking their positions and trying to suppress planned protests. Police said they were conducting a security operation against armed militias, while the UN, US embassy, EU and regional bloc IGAD condemned the violence and urged restraint and dialogue.

stories.headline: Russia attacks Kyiv as Ukraine reports gains and strikes in Crimea
stories.body: Russia launched heavy attacks on Kyiv, setting fires in residential and non-residential areas and sending residents into shelters after warning it would strike Ukrainian military-linked and decision-making targets in the capital. At the same time, Ukraine has expanded drone and missile strikes against Russian supply routes, energy infrastructure and military sites in occupied Crimea and inside Russia, with Moscow-installed officials saying strikes in Crimea killed at least four people, including in Simferopol and near Kerch. Ukrainian and outside analysts say Kyiv has made small net territorial gains for a second consecutive month and is using longer-range drones to pressure Russian logistics, fuel supplies and command infrastructure, while President Volodymyr Zelensky urged more international pressure on Moscow to resume negotiations before winter.

stories.headline: US House votes to curb Trump’s Iran war powers as talks with Tehran stall
stories.body: The US House voted 215-208 to approve a war powers resolution requiring President Donald Trump to seek congressional authorization for the war with Iran or withdraw US forces, with four Republicans joining Democrats in a rare rebuke of the president. The vote came more than 90 days into the conflict, a threshold Democrats argue triggers the 1973 War Powers Resolution, though the White House says intermittent ceasefires and the nature of the operations mean congressional approval is not required. The measure now goes to the Senate and may remain largely symbolic because of legal uncertainty and Trump’s veto power, but it reflects growing Republican unease as peace talks with Tehran remain stalled, the Strait of Hormuz stays closed and US-Iran hostilities continue to disrupt global trade.

stories.headline: Trump administration separated dozens of immigrant children from parents for a second time, AP finds
stories.body: An Associated Press investigation found that the Trump administration has re-separated dozens of immigrant children from parents who had already been separated under Trump’s first-term border policy, despite a court settlement meant to protect those families from being separated again until 2031. Some parents have been detained for months or deported after interior immigration arrests, and in some cases officials reportedly removed people despite discovering they were legally off limits for deportation. The separations differ from 2018, when children were forcibly taken at the border, but advocates say parents arrested or deported under the administration’s mass-deportation push are now being forced to choose whether to leave children behind in the United States, retraumatizing families covered by the earlier settlement.

stories.headline: Support for same-sex marriage and transgender rights shifts sharply in new polling and lawsuits
stories.body: A new Gallup poll shows US support for same-sex marriage has declined to 65% from a recent high of 71%, driven mainly by a sharp drop among Republicans, whose support has fallen from 55% in 2022 to 37%. Gallup also found support for the moral acceptability of same-sex relationships and gender transition has fallen, with only 38% of Americans now saying changing genders is morally acceptable and Republican support dropping to 5%. The polling reflects a widening partisan divide that is also visible in state-level policy fights, including bills in at least 11 states seeking to challenge or restrict recognition of same-sex marriage despite the Supreme Court’s 2015 nationwide ruling.

stories.headline: New bipartisan AI bill lands in Congress as OpenAI splits with White House on safety rules
stories.body: Representatives Jay Obernolte and Lori Trahan are introducing a bipartisan AI bill that would require leading AI developers to disclose safety and security risks for frontier models and submit that information to third-party auditors, while temporarily limiting states’ ability to regulate how top AI companies develop the technology. The state preemption language has drawn criticism from AI safety advocates and tech critics, even as the bill represents one of Congress’s last major chances to pass federal AI rules before the midterms. Separately, OpenAI proposed mandatory federal evaluations of advanced AI models led by civilian agencies such as NIST’s Center for AI Standards and Innovation, diverging from a new Trump executive order that relies on voluntary vetting and gives the NSA a leading role in cybersecurity risk evaluation.

stories.headline: Spain’s Leire Díez scandal widens with Guardia Civil claims, UCO searches and pressure on Sánchez allies
stories.body: The investigation into former PSOE militant Leire Díez widened as case files and audio recordings described meetings with Guardia Civil officers, prosecutors, politicians, business figures under investigation and former police commissioner José Manuel Villarejo, allegedly aimed at undermining corruption cases affecting the Socialist Party and people close to Prime Minister Pedro Sánchez. One recording reportedly shows Díez describing Guardia Civil director Mercedes González as someone she trusted, while Guardia Civil UCO officers searched Tubos Reunidos offices in Álava and Bilbao as part of a sealed branch of the case. Sánchez’s coalition partners in Sumar demanded explanations “at the highest level,” transparency and accountability, while reporting from the case file points to alleged links with former PSOE organization secretary Santos Cerdán and efforts to obtain compromising material on investigators, judges and prosecutors.

stories.headline: David Sánchez denies using his surname to influence public job case
stories.body: David Sánchez, the brother of Prime Minister Pedro Sánchez, denied in court that he used his surname or had any capacity to influence the creation or award of public posts at the Badajoz provincial government, where he is accused of influence peddling and prevarication over his 2017 appointment and the 2023 hiring of a friend. His testimony lasted about 10 minutes and he answered only his lawyer’s questions, focusing on his later job change and denying that he performed private work without authorization. His defense argued that charges tied to his original appointment should be dropped because related allegations are time-barred, while popular accusations led by PP, Vox and far-right groups maintain that the position was created for him and are seeking prison time and disqualification from office.

stories.headline: Catalan teachers reject pay agreement and unions call new strikes as government says it will proceed
stories.body: Catalan public school teachers rejected a proposed agreement with the Education Department, with 65.1% of participating teachers voting no in a consultation that drew more than 60,000 ballots, or 61% of the eligible workforce. The deal included a new monthly pay supplement phased in over four years, 6,400 positions to strengthen inclusive education and new secondary-school posts, but USTEC withdrew its support after the vote and joined CGT and Intersindical in calling a strike and further mobilizations, including possible action around Pope Leo XIV’s visit and university entrance exams. Education minister Esther Niubó said the government would still move forward with the measures because another signatory union, Professors de Secundària, backed the agreement internally, while ERC and Comuns urged the government to reopen negotiations.

stories.headline: Catalonia’s budget clears first parliamentary hurdle amid education conflict
stories.body: Catalonia’s 2026 budget cleared its first parliamentary hurdle after PSC, ERC and Comuns voted down total-rejection amendments from Junts, PP, Vox, CUP and Aliança Catalana, keeping Salvador Illa’s first budget on track for a final vote expected in early July. Economy minister Alícia Romero praised ERC and Comuns for enabling nearly €50 billion in spending after years of extended budgets, while urging Junts and PP not to block a new regional financing deal in Spain’s Congress. The vote was overshadowed by the teachers’ rejection of the education agreement, with ERC and Comuns defending their budget pact as preferable to continued deadlock but pressing the government to make a renewed effort to resolve the school conflict.

stories.headline: Heavy rain alerts expanded across Catalonia as Civil Protection warns of flash river rises
stories.body: Catalonia’s meteorological service expanded high-risk rain alerts to 17 counties and moderate alerts to many more as Civil Protection kept the Inuncat flood plan activated, warning of intense and sudden storms across parts of Barcelona, Girona and the northeast. Forecasters said rainfall could exceed 40 liters per square meter in 30 minutes and, under a newly issued three-hour warning, reach 60 liters per square meter in parts of Ripollès, Garrotxa, Osona and Lluçanès, with storms possibly accompanied by hail. Because recent rains have left soils saturated, authorities warned that rivers and streams could rise quickly and urged residents to avoid flood-prone areas and never cross rivers, streams or ravines even if little water is visible.

stories.headline: Pope Leo XIV’s Barcelona visit stirs debate over Catalan at Sagrada Família blessing
stories.body: Barcelona archbishop Joan Josep Omella said Pope Leo XIV will say “some words” in Catalan during the blessing of the Sagrada Família’s Tower of Jesus, seeking to defuse controversy after reports that the central liturgical act of the pope’s June 9-11 visit would be conducted only in Spanish. Omella called the dispute exaggerated and said the pope knows Catalan is spoken in Catalonia, is sensitive to languages and has been rehearsing Catalan text, while organizers said other events, including a prayer at Barcelona Cathedral, will be largely in Catalan with some Spanish. The issue has drawn criticism from Catalan political and civic figures, including Carles Puigdemont, and a public letter from former leaders and organizations demanding a visible role for Catalan during the visit.
